proposals.title: REPLUG-DT: Generalizing the REPLUG Framework for Diverse Task Adaptation via Implicit Query and Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: The REPLUG framework has demonstrated strong performance in language modeling by treating retrieval as a plug-in module, but its application has been largely confined to perplexity reduction. Extending its principles to complex, open-ended tasks like open-domain QA and dialogue remains an open challenge, crucial for building more grounded and knowledge-aware language agents. This research addresses the gap between retrieval-augmented language models and their effective, task-agnostic deployment across diverse downstream applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core REPLUG principle of training a retriever to optimize the end LM's likelihood can be effectively generalized to diverse task objectives by integrating implicit query formulation and reinforcement learning, leading to significant performance gains over both standard fine-tuning and task-specific RAG systems.
proposals.method: We will adapt the REPLUG architecture, where a frozen LM scores documents retrieved by a trainable dense retriever. For task adaptation, we will develop a two-stage training process: 1) Use an implicit query generation module (inspired by ImpRAG) to derive task-specific retrieval signals from the LM's hidden states. 2) Employ a policy gradient reinforcement learning objective (inspired by SCRIBES) to directly optimize the retriever for final task reward (e.g., QA accuracy, dialogue coherence), rather than just language modeling likelihood. We will benchmark on datasets like Natural Questions and DailyDialog.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, task-agnostic framework for applying retrieval-augmentation to diverse NLP tasks, moving beyond language modeling. It will yield empirical results demonstrating the transferability of the REPLUG paradigm and offer new insights into training retrievers with indirect supervision from frozen, large LMs for end-task performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of the REPLUG training paradigm with implicit query mechanisms and RL-based task optimization, creating a general-purpose retrieval-augmentation system not limited to a single task or requiring LM parameter updates.

proposals.title: Beyond Likelihood: Training Retrieval Models with Contrastive and Implicit Feedback for RAG
proposals.motivation: In Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), the retrieval model is typically trained using the likelihood of the target text given the retrieved context, a signal that can be noisy and misaligned with downstream task success. This reliance on a single, often brittle, supervision signal limits the robustness and generalization of retrievers, especially in complex, multi-step reasoning tasks. Exploring alternative, more direct supervision signals is crucial for building retrieval systems that are more reliable and better integrated with the generator's needs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a dual-encoder retriever using a combination of contrastive learning on implicit query representations and direct reinforcement learning rewards from task completion will yield a retrieval model that outperforms likelihood-trained models on complex, knowledge-intensive generation tasks.
proposals.method: We will build on the Implicit Query framework from ImpRAG and the RL-based extraction approach from SCRIBES. First, we will generate implicit query embeddings from the generator's intermediate layers. We will train the retriever using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) to align these implicit queries with relevant document embeddings. Concurrently, we will fine-tune the retriever with a policy gradient method, where the reward is a weighted combination of task-specific metrics (e.g., answer correctness, code execution success) and a consistency penalty based on the generator's output probability when using the retrieved documents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, hybrid training paradigm for retrieval models that directly optimizes for downstream utility rather than proxy likelihood objectives. It will deliver a robust, task-aware retriever that improves the performance of RAG systems on benchmarks requiring deep knowledge integration and reasoning, such as complex QA and code generation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses either contrastive learning (e.g., DPR) or RL for retrieval, our method uniquely integrates contrastive alignment on *implicit*, dynamically generated queries with RL rewards from the end-to-end RAG loop, creating a more direct and stable learning signal for the retriever.

proposals.title: DiverseScale: Scaling and Diversifying Training Data for Robust Neural Retrievers
proposals.motivation: The performance of dense retrieval models is heavily dependent on the quality and scale of their training data, typically limited to curated datasets like MS MARCO. This creates a bottleneck for generalization, especially for tasks requiring diverse query formulations or access to specialized domains. Scaling training data in a principled way is a critical, unsolved challenge for building truly robust and general-purpose retrieval systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training dense retrievers on a large-scale, synthetically diversified corpus of query-document pairs, generated by large language models (LLMs), will significantly improve their zero-shot and out-of-domain retrieval robustness compared to models trained on existing human-annotated datasets alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage a multi-stage pipeline inspired by the researcher's prior work. First, we will use a powerful, instruction-tuned LLM (e.g., GPT-4) to perform SCRIBES-like script-based extraction and rewriting of queries from a massive web corpus, creating diverse paraphrases and implicit query formulations. Second, we will apply Bridge-Coder's principle of capability transfer by using the LLM to generate pseudo-relevant query-document pairs for low-resource domains. Finally, we will train a dual-encoder retriever (e.g., Contriever, DPR) on this combined synthetic dataset, potentially using ImpRAG's implicit query framework to handle the varied query structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable methodology for creating high-quality retrieval training data, a large-scale open-source synthetic dataset, and empirical evidence on the limits and benefits of data scaling for neural retrievers. It directly addresses a key bottleneck in moving retrieval systems beyond narrow, in-domain benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs for data augmentation, our approach systematically combines structured extraction (SCRIBES), cross-domain transfer (Bridge-Coder), and implicit query modeling (ImpRAG) to generate a massive and *strategically* diverse training corpus, moving beyond simple paraphrasing or domain adaptation in isolation.

proposals.title: Contrastive Retrieval Augmentation: Improving RAG with Dense Retriever Fine-tuning via Multi-Granularity Contrastive Learning
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, a core theme in your recent work like ImpRAG, critically depend on the precision of the retriever. Pre-trained dense retrievers often underperform in specialized domains or tasks due to a mismatch between their pre-training objectives and the downstream retrieval needs. This leads to irrelevant context being passed to the generator, degrading overall system accuracy and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: Fine-tuning a dense retriever using a multi-granularity contrastive loss function, which jointly optimizes for query-document and query-chunk alignment, will significantly improve its discrimination for task-specific RAG, leading to measurable gains in end-task performance.
proposals.method: We propose to fine-tune a pre-trained encoder (e.g., Contriever or ANCE) using a novel contrastive objective. For a given query, we will sample positive and negative documents from the task corpus. Within positive documents, we will also identify relevant chunks (e.g., sentences or paragraphs). The loss will combine a standard InfoNCE loss at the document level with an additional fine-grained loss that pulls the query representation closer to representations of its relevant chunks than to irrelevant chunks from the same or other documents. This dual-objective training will be integrated into an end-to-end RAG pipeline for evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, task-adaptive fine-tuning methodology for dense retrievers within RAG frameworks. It will yield a publicly available model and demonstrate quantifiable improvements in retrieval metrics (Recall@k, MRR) and downstream QA/generation accuracy on benchmarks like Natural Questions and HotpotQA, advancing the state of task-specific retrieval.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established for retrieval, its application within RAG has focused on query-document pairs. Our approach innovates by explicitly modeling the intra-document chunk-level relevance during retriever fine-tuning, a granularity crucial for generator performance but often overlooked in pre-2026 retriever training paradigms.

proposals.title: CurriculumRAG: Progressive Difficulty Training for Robust Neural Retrievers
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems critically depend on the quality of the retriever, yet dense retrievers are often trained on static, uniformly difficult datasets, leading to suboptimal generalization and robustness on complex, multi-hop queries. Curriculum learning, which sequences training data from easy to hard, has proven effective in other deep learning domains but remains underexplored for modern dual-encoder and late-interaction retrievers. This research aims to systematically improve retriever training dynamics and final performance by adapting this powerful paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training dense passage retrievers using a curriculum of progressively harder query-document pairs will lead to faster convergence, better final accuracy on complex retrieval tasks, and improved generalization compared to standard random-batch training.
proposals.method: We will design a difficulty metric for query-document pairs, combining lexical similarity (e.g., BM25), semantic complexity of the query, and the granularity of the answer signal in the document. Using this metric, we will construct a training curriculum for models like DPR or ANCE, starting with high-similarity, factoid examples and progressing to low-similarity, multi-hop or implicit queries. We will experiment with both predefined static curricula and dynamic, loss-driven scheduling (e.g., Self-Paced Learning). The retriever will be evaluated within a full RAG pipeline and in isolation on benchmarks like HotpotQA and BEIR.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training framework for neural retrievers, demonstrating measurable gains in accuracy and robustness. It will yield insights into the optimization landscape of retrieval models and produce a publicly available toolkit for curriculum-based retriever training, extending the applicability of RAG systems to more challenging domains.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is established, its application to modern dual-encoder retrievers trained with contrastive loss is novel. The core innovation lies in defining and scheduling difficulty specifically for the retrieval task, moving beyond simple heuristics to metrics that reflect the nuanced relationship between query formulation and document relevance.

proposals.title: Dual-Eval: Benchmarking Retrieval Models via Generative Consistency with Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Evaluating retrieval models often relies on static, human-annotated relevance judgments, which are expensive to create and struggle to adapt to new domains or nuanced information needs. As retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems become central to NLP, we need more flexible, automatic methods to assess retrieval quality directly in the context of downstream generative tasks. This work addresses the gap by using the generative capability of LLMs themselves as a dynamic evaluation tool.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the consistency between an LLM's answers generated from a ground-truth document and from a retriever's top results provides a robust, task-aware metric for retrieval quality, correlating strongly with human judgment and downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where, for a query, an LLM (e.g., GPT-4, Llama 2) first generates a reference answer using a known relevant document. Concurrently, a candidate retriever fetches a set of passages. The same LLM then generates an answer using only these retrieved passages. We will compute consistency scores using both n-gram overlap (ROUGE) and semantic similarity (BERTScore) between the two answers. We will validate this 'Dual-Eval' metric against human relevance labels on established benchmarks (e.g., BEIR, MS MARCO) and its correlation with final accuracy in RAG pipelines for QA.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, automatic evaluation paradigm for retrieval models that is directly aligned with generative utility, reducing reliance on static annotations. We will release a benchmark suite and analysis showing how different retriever failures (e.g., recall, precision) manifest in generative consistency, providing diagnostic insights for improving RAG systems.
proposals.novelty: While LLMs are commonly used to generate synthetic data or as RAG components, using them as referees to *evaluate* retrievers based on answer consistency is a novel role reversal. This approach moves beyond traditional retrieval metrics like NDCG to a task-intrinsic, model-based evaluation.

proposals.title: Specialized Reviewers for Instruction Reconstruction: A Task-Specific Fine-Tuning and Retrieval-Augmented Generation Approach
proposals.motivation: Instruction-following models rely on high-quality, diverse training data, often generated by a two-stage process where a Writer generates instructions and a Reviewer reconstructs them. However, generic Reviewer models may be suboptimal for this specific reconstruction task, limiting the quality and efficiency of synthetic data generation. This research addresses a critical bottleneck in scaling instruction tuning by investigating whether specialized Reviewers can significantly improve reconstruction fidelity and downstream model performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Fine-tuning a general-purpose language model specifically for the instruction-reconstruction task will yield a Reviewer that outperforms generic models in reconstruction accuracy and efficiency, and that augmenting this model with a retrieval mechanism for similar instruction-response pairs will further enhance its performance.
proposals.method: We will first create a high-quality dataset of (instruction, response, reconstructed instruction) triplets. Using this dataset, we will fine-tune a base LLM (e.g., LLaMA or T5) to specialize as a Reviewer. Concurrently, we will develop a retrieval-augmented Reviewer (RAR) model, inspired by the Implicit Query approach of ImpRAG, which retrieves relevant examples from a corpus of instructions to condition the reconstruction. We will compare the performance of the fine-tuned model, the RAR model, and a baseline generic model using metrics like BLEU, ROUGE, and human evaluation of reconstruction quality and downstream task performance of Writers trained on the generated data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic analysis of task-specific specialization for data generation components in the LLM training pipeline. It will deliver a novel, open-sourced Reviewer model and a retrieval-augmented framework for instruction reconstruction, potentially setting a new standard for synthetic data generation in instruction tuning. The findings will inform best practices for creating efficient, high-quality training data at scale.
proposals.novelty: While fine-tuning for downstream tasks is common, applying targeted specialization to an intermediate, data-generating component like a Reviewer is an underexplored area. Furthermore, integrating retrieval mechanisms specifically to improve instruction reconstruction, rather than direct question answering, is a novel application of RAG principles.

proposals.title: DualCoder: Systematic Instruction Generation from Code for Data Augmentation and Model Diagnosis
proposals.motivation: While code generation from natural language instructions has been a major focus of LLM research, the inverse task—generating high-quality instructions from code—remains underexplored. This capability is crucial for creating synthetic training data for low-resource programming languages and for diagnosing the instruction-following biases of code generation models. A systematic study of this dual problem can improve data efficiency and model interpretability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) model, trained to produce diverse and precise natural language instructions from code, can generate high-quality synthetic data that improves the performance of code generation models, especially for low-resource languages, and can reveal the implicit assumptions models make about user intent.
proposals.method: We will develop DualCoder, a T5 or CodeT5-based encoder-decoder model. Inspired by ImpRAG, we will use an implicit retrieval mechanism where the model's encoder produces a query vector to fetch relevant code-instruction pairs from a web-scale corpus (like Stack Overflow) to condition the decoder. Training will leverage reinforcement learning, similar to SCRIBES, with rewards for instruction clarity, diversity, and faithfulness to the code's semantics. We will apply Bridge-Coder's transfer learning principles to adapt the model from high-resource (Python) to low-resource languages.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel methodology and benchmark for the code-to-instruction task, 2) a data augmentation pipeline to improve code generation LLMs, and 3) a diagnostic tool to analyze the alignment between model-generated code and human intent by comparing original and model-inferred instructions.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely frames instruction generation as a systematic, model-agnostic data augmentation and diagnostic tool, moving beyond simple captioning. It innovatively combines RAG with implicit querying and RL-based training—techniques proven in the researcher's prior work for other domains—specifically for this inverse programming problem.

proposals.title: Cross-Context Code Infilling: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Multi-File and Multi-Language Programming Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current code generation and infilling models, such as those based on the single-file, monolingual paradigm of Codex, are fundamentally limited when addressing real-world software development. Modern codebases are inherently multi-file and often polyglot, requiring understanding of cross-file dependencies, APIs, and data schemas. This gap leads to models that generate syntactically correct but semantically incoherent code when context is restricted to a single file or language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework, equipped with a specialized retriever trained to gather relevant context from a broader codebase (including multiple files and languages), will significantly improve the semantic accuracy and developer utility of code infilling models compared to single-context baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage model inspired by the RAG and transfer learning themes in the researcher's prior work (e.g., ImpRAG, Bridge-Coder). First, we will train a dense retriever using contrastive learning on code graph data (e.g., call graphs, import graphs) to fetch relevant cross-file and cross-language contexts given an infilling prompt. Second, we will fine-tune a large language model (LLM) for infilling using a fusion-in-decoder architecture, where the retrieved contexts are concatenated with the local prompt. Training will leverage web-scale semi-structured code data, applying reinforcement learning with correctness rewards (similar to SCRIBES) to optimize for functional program correctness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new benchmark for evaluating cross-context code infilling, 2) a novel retriever architecture specialized for intra-repository code relationships, and 3) an empirically validated model that demonstrates the necessity of broad context for practical code assistance tools, moving beyond the single-file assumption.
proposals.novelty: While RAG is known for factual knowledge, its application to structured, semantic retrieval within a codebase for the specific task of infilling is novel. Furthermore, explicitly training a model to leverage and reason over multi-language context for a single generation task goes beyond prior transfer learning approaches that focus on mapping between languages.

proposals.title: Bidirectional Infilling for Structured Data Generation and Augmentation
proposals.motivation: Bidirectional infilling objectives, as seen in models like T5 and BART, have proven powerful for natural language tasks by learning to reconstruct corrupted spans from bidirectional context. However, their application to structured sequential data—such as code, JSON, or semi-structured web tables—remains underexplored. Extending this paradigm to structured data could significantly improve model robustness for tasks like data imputation, schema-consistent generation, and script-based information extraction, which are central to building reliable data-centric AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a transformer-based model with a structured corruption and bidirectional infilling objective on diverse sequential structured data will yield a foundation model that outperforms standard left-to-right or masked language modeling approaches on tasks requiring schema understanding and data completion.
proposals.method: We will adapt the T5-style span corruption objective to structured data types, designing corruption strategies that respect syntactic boundaries (e.g., JSON brackets, code blocks, table cells). A transformer encoder-decoder will be trained to reconstruct these spans. For evaluation, we will fine-tune this pre-trained model on downstream tasks from the researcher's prior work, including semi-structured data extraction (SCRIBES) and code generation for low-resource languages (Bridge-Coder), comparing against baselines trained with standard objectives. We will also explore integrating implicit retrieval (ImpRAG) to condition infilling on external knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new pre-training methodology tailored for structured data, a benchmark for evaluating structured infilling, and demonstrate improved performance on practical data wrangling and code generation tasks. It will provide a unified framework for handling gaps and noise in real-world structured sequences.
proposals.novelty: While infilling is established for text, its systematic adaptation to the syntax and semantics of diverse structured data formats is novel. The integration of this training objective with retrieval for context-aware structured generation moves beyond prior work focused solely on natural language or single data types.

proposals.title: CodePolyglot: Scaling Cross-Lingual Code Model Training with Web-Scale Data Curation and Implicit Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Current large language models for code are predominantly trained on high-resource languages like Python and JavaScript, creating a significant capability gap for low-resource programming languages. This limits the democratization of software development tools and hinders the maintenance of legacy systems. Scaling training with a vastly expanded, multi-lingual corpus is a direct path to bridging this gap, as suggested by prior work like Bridge-Coder, but requires novel methods for data acquisition and efficient learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a significantly larger transformer model on a meticulously curated, web-scale multi-lingual code corpus will lead to emergent cross-lingual transfer capabilities, enabling state-of-the-art performance on low-resource language tasks without task-specific supervision. Furthermore, we posit that integrating implicit retrieval-augmented generation (ImpRAG) during fine-tuning will efficiently specialize this scaled model for complex, knowledge-intensive coding tasks.
proposals.method: We will first construct CodePolyglot-Corpus, a massive dataset using SCRIBES-inspired reinforcement learning agents to extract and clean semi-structured code from diverse web sources, prioritizing coverage of low-resource languages. We will then pre-train a decoder-only transformer (e.g., 70B+ parameters) on this corpus using next-token prediction. Finally, for task adaptation, we will employ an ImpRAG-style fine-tuning stage where the model learns to generate implicit queries to retrieve and integrate relevant code snippets from its own training corpus or a knowledge base, enhancing its reasoning without explicit retrieval instructions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) the CodePolyglot model, a new state-of-the-art foundation model for code with strong multi-lingual abilities; 2) the methodology and analysis for scaling code data curation and training; and 3) a demonstration of how ImpRAG mechanisms can be integrated into large-scale pre-training for improved task performance, advancing the field of retrieval-augmented generation for code.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic scaling of both data (via automated, RL-based web extraction for low-resource languages) and model size specifically for code, combined with the integration of implicit retrieval-augmentation into the fine-tuning of such a scaled model, moving beyond simple next-token prediction for complex task solving.

proposals.title: Exe2NL: Generating Natural Language Documentation from Code and Execution Traces via Implicit Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Automatically generating accurate natural language descriptions for code is crucial for software maintenance and developer onboarding. Existing methods primarily rely on static code analysis, missing the rich semantic context revealed by program execution. This gap limits the quality and actionability of generated documentation, especially for complex or data-dependent logic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a retrieval-augmented generation model, conditioned on both static code and dynamic execution results, can produce more precise and contextually relevant natural language descriptions than models using code alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Implicit Query mechanism from ImpRAG to build a dual-encoder architecture. One encoder processes the static source code, while a second encoder processes structured execution traces (e.g., variable states, I/O). An implicit query module will fuse these representations to retrieve relevant documentation snippets from a web-scale corpus, similar to the SCRIBES pipeline. This retrieved context, along with the original code and execution data, will be fed into a decoder (based on a large language model like Codex or CodeGen) to generate the final natural language description. Reinforcement learning, as explored in SCRIBES, will be used to optimize for description accuracy and readability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for execution-aware code documentation generation and a new dataset pairing code snippets with execution traces and human-written descriptions. It will advance the understanding of how dynamic program semantics can be integrated into NL generation tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on code summarization, our method explicitly incorporates execution results as a conditioning signal. Furthermore, it uniquely combines the implicit retrieval paradigm of ImpRAG with the structured data extraction scale of SCRIBES for this inverse problem.

proposals.title: Query-Aware Attention for Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Learning to Weight Informative Question Tokens
proposals.motivation: In Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), the retrieval step is critical for providing relevant context. Standard RAG models treat all tokens in a user query uniformly when computing a dense representation for retrieval, but this is suboptimal; function words or common phrases can dilute the representation of key informative terms. This leads to irrelevant document retrieval, propagating errors to the generation phase and degrading overall system performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learning to assign non-uniform, informativeness-based weights to query tokens during the retrieval phase will produce more discriminative query embeddings, leading to higher precision in retrieved documents and improved final answer quality in RAG systems.
proposals.method: We propose a lightweight, trainable gating module that operates on the encoder output of a frozen query encoder (e.g., Contriever or BERT). This module will compute a scalar weight for each token using a learned function of its contextual embedding, potentially leveraging reinforcement learning signals from downstream answer quality (inspired by SCRIBES) or supervised signals from retrieval relevance. The weighted token embeddings are then aggregated (e.g., via weighted sum) to form the final query vector for dense retrieval. We will integrate and evaluate this within an end-to-end RAG framework like the one explored in ImpRAG.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, parameter-efficient method for improving the retrieval interface in RAG systems. It will provide empirical evidence on multiple QA benchmarks that token-aware retrieval outperforms standard uniform encoding. The proposed gating module offers a general, plug-and-play component for enhancing pre-trained retrievers without full fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like ImpRAG explores implicit query refinement, our approach explicitly learns to re-weight the original query tokens at the granular token level for the specific task of retrieval, a focused intervention distinct from post-retrieval query generation or document re-ranking.

proposals.title: CLASP: Contrastive Learning for Aligning Semantics in dense Passage retrieval
proposals.motivation: Dense retrieval models, crucial for RAG systems, are typically trained with pointwise or pairwise losses that struggle with fine-grained semantic distinctions and hard negative mining. Pre-2026, self-supervised contrastive learning in vision (e.g., SimCLR, MoCo) has proven highly effective at learning robust representations by pulling augmented views of the same image together while pushing others apart, but its principles are underexplored for aligning heterogeneous text modalities like queries and passages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting vision-style contrastive learning frameworks, with tailored text augmentations and hard negative sampling strategies, will yield a more robust and semantically aligned joint query-passage embedding space than standard triplet or NLL losses, improving retrieval accuracy for knowledge-intensive tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Momentum Contrast (MoCo) framework to text. A query encoder and a momentum-updated passage encoder will produce embeddings. Positive pairs will be (query, relevant passage), with in-batch and a dynamically maintained queue of passage negatives. We will design semantic-preserving text augmentations (e.g., back-translation, entity masking) to generate additional positive views for queries, applying an InfoNCE loss. Training will leverage large-scale web data (like in SCRIBES) and knowledge-intensive QA datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training paradigm for dense retrievers, demonstrating the transferability of vision-based contrastive principles to cross-modal text alignment. It will provide empirical evidence on improved robustness to hard negatives and semantic granularity, advancing the foundation for more effective RAG systems as seen in ImpRAG.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is known in NLP, directly porting the specific architectures and augmentation philosophies from state-of-the-art vision models (MoCo) to the asymmetric query-passage retrieval problem is novel. The core innovation lies in treating the query as an 'augmented view' of the relevant passage's semantic core.

proposals.title: Iterative Query Generation for Scalable Multi-Hop Reasoning in Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: Complex question answering often requires multi-hop reasoning across disparate pieces of evidence, a task where current dense retrieval systems struggle with cascading errors. While retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has improved factual grounding, its single-step retrieval is insufficient for queries demanding iterative evidence gathering. Scaling multi-hop reasoning to large, heterogeneous corpora remains a significant open challenge in pre-2026 NLP.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative retrieval process, driven by a learned query generator (QG) re-ranker that refines queries based on intermediate context, will significantly improve the precision and recall of evidence chains for multi-hop questions compared to single-step or naive iterative retrieval.
proposals.method: We propose a novel system that treats multi-hop retrieval as a partially observable Markov decision process. Building on the QG re-ranker concept from prior work, we will train a transformer-based agent to perform iterative retrieval: given a question and a history of retrieved documents, the agent generates an implicit or explicit query for the next hop. This query is executed against a dense index (e.g., using DPR or Contriever), and the resulting documents are re-ranked and added to the context. The process repeats for a fixed number of hops or until a confidence threshold is met, with the final evidence chain fed to a generator (e.g., T5 or an LLM) for answer synthesis. Training will leverage reinforcement learning with rewards for answer correctness and document relevance, similar to SCRIBES, applied on datasets like HotpotQA and MuSiQue.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable, trainable framework for multi-hop RAG that explicitly models the iterative nature of evidence retrieval. It will provide new insights into how retrieval systems can be guided by intermediate context and demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on complex QA benchmarks requiring reasoning over large knowledge sources.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of a learned, context-aware query generator as the core controller for a multi-step retrieval agent, moving beyond static query expansion or rule-based decomposition. This creates a closed-loop, adaptive retrieval process distinct from prior multi-stage RAG systems.

proposals.title: Retrieval-Augmented Question Generation: Training and Evaluating with Contextual Passages
proposals.motivation: Current question generation (QG) models often produce generic or contextually shallow questions because they rely solely on a single source document or answer span. This limits their utility for creating high-quality training data for QA systems or educational applications. By integrating retrieved passages as additional context, we can ground QG in a richer, multi-document knowledge base, mimicking how humans formulate questions by synthesizing information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using a set of retrieved, relevant passages as context for QG models will lead to the generation of more specific, diverse, and answerable questions compared to models using only a single source document.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-phase framework. First, for any given answer or topic, we use a dense retriever (e.g., DPR or Contriever) to fetch a small set of relevant passages from a large corpus (e.g., Wikipedia). Second, we adapt a sequence-to-sequence model (e.g., T5 or BART) to condition its question generation on this retrieved context, potentially using a fusion-in-decoder architecture. We will train this model using a reinforcement learning objective, inspired by SCRIBES, to optimize for question quality metrics like answerability, diversity, and relevance, evaluated against the retrieved evidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel retrieval-augmented QG framework and a corresponding evaluation protocol that assesses question quality against retrieved evidence. It will advance the state of the art in generating high-quality, knowledge-grounded questions, directly benefiting downstream tasks like QA system training and educational content creation.
proposals.novelty: While retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is established for QA, its application to *control and improve* QG is less explored. Our approach uniquely uses retrieval not just for evaluation but as core, trainable context, and leverages RL for direct optimization of multi-faceted question quality.

proposals.title: WebInstruct: Scaling Instruction-Tuning to Web-Scale Task Mixtures via Implicit Retrieval and Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Current instruction-tuned models like INSTRUCTOR are trained on curated, high-quality datasets, which limits their exposure to the true diversity and scale of tasks found on the web. Scaling instruction-following capabilities to handle the long-tail of real-world, semi-structured tasks is crucial for developing more general and robust AI assistants. This work addresses the challenge of moving from curated benchmarks to web-scale, noisy instruction-task mixtures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training an instruction-following model on orders of magnitude more tasks, automatically extracted from web data, will yield significant improvements in generalization, robustness, and task compositionality compared to models trained on curated sets.
proposals.method: We will leverage and extend methods from the researcher's prior work: 1) Use SCRIBES-style reinforcement learning to extract a massive, diverse corpus of (instruction, output) pairs from semi-structured web data (e.g., tutorials, how-to guides, API docs). 2) Apply Bridge-Coder-inspired transfer techniques to propagate capabilities from high-resource to low-resource task types within this corpus. 3) Integrate ImpRAG's implicit query mechanism during training, allowing the model to internally retrieve and compose relevant knowledge from the massive, noisy task mixture for each instruction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new state-of-the-art instruction-following model trained on an unprecedented scale and diversity of tasks. It will provide a methodology for scalable, web-driven instruction tuning and a benchmark to measure robustness on noisy, long-tail instructions. The research will advance understanding of how scale and diversity in task mixtures affect model generalization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic application of web-scale extraction (SCRIBES), capability transfer (Bridge-Coder), and implicit retrieval (ImpRAG) specifically for scaling instruction tuning, moving beyond curated datasets to learn directly from the web's inherent task structure.

proposals.title: Instruct-Embed: Learning Instruction-Guided Unified Embeddings for Multimodal Retrieval and Composition
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models often rely on fixed, task-specific alignment strategies, lacking the flexibility to adapt to diverse downstream tasks through natural language instructions. This limits their application in dynamic, open-ended scenarios like complex cross-modal retrieval or instruction-based content generation. A unified embedding space that can be dynamically shaped by instructions would enable more versatile and controllable multimodal systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single transformer-based model can be trained to project text, images, and audio into a shared embedding space where the geometric relationships are explicitly modulated by a natural language instruction, enabling superior performance on instruction-sensitive multimodal tasks compared to fixed-alignment models.
proposals.method: We will extend the instruction-tuning paradigm to multimodal embeddings by designing a novel architecture based on a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP-ViT) and audio encoder. A cross-modal transformer will process concatenated instruction, text, and modality-specific features. The core training objective combines a contrastive loss (aligning embeddings of related instruction-modality pairs) with a novel instruction-following loss that measures whether the embedding can correctly retrieve or rank outputs based on the instruction's intent, using datasets like LAION with synthetic instructions and task-specific benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for instruction-aware multimodal representation learning, providing a single model capable of powering diverse tasks like compositional retrieval, zero-shot cross-modal classification, and instruction-based asset composition. It will advance the field beyond static alignment towards controllable, task-adaptive embeddings.
proposals.novelty: While instruction-tuning is established in language models and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), applying it to learn a *unified, instruction-steerable embedding space* for multiple non-textual modalities is a novel direction, distinct from prior work on fixed multimodal encoders or instruction-following generative models.

proposals.title: FlowRAG: End-to-End Generative Answer Synthesis with Implicit Evidence Flow
proposals.motivation: Current retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, including extractive span-based models, treat evidence retrieval and answer generation as loosely coupled stages. This pipeline approach can lead to brittle, contextually narrow answers and struggles with tasks requiring synthesis, explanation, or multi-step reasoning from multiple documents. A mechanism for fluid, end-to-end generation grounded in evidence is a critical missing piece for robust, human-like question answering.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the flow of information from a retrieved evidence set to a free-form answer as a differentiable, end-to-end process, we can surpass the performance and coherence of explicit span extraction and standard RAG pipelines on complex QA tasks.
proposals.method: We propose FlowRAG, a novel architecture built upon a pre-trained encoder-decoder LLM. We introduce a 'Flow Transformer' layer that sits between the encoder (processing retrieved passages) and the decoder (generating the answer). This layer uses cross-attention and a novel gated recurrent mechanism to iteratively construct a dynamic, implicit 'evidence flow' representation, which the decoder attends to for generation. Training will leverage a combination of maximum likelihood estimation on datasets like ELI5 and Natural Questions, and reinforcement learning with a reward function blending answer fidelity (via NLI models) and fluency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new neural architecture for generative QA that eliminates the need for explicit answer spans, 2) a public benchmark for evaluating generative answer synthesis, and 3) empirical evidence showing superior performance on explanation-style and multi-hop QA tasks compared to extractive and standard generative RAG baselines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like ImpRAG explores implicit queries for retrieval, FlowRAG uniquely focuses on an implicit, learnable flow mechanism for answer synthesis *after* retrieval, moving beyond simple concatenation of evidence or reliance on explicit span supervision.

proposals.title: Multi-Modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Video and Audio Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems excel in text but struggle with the high-dimensional, sequential nature of video and audio. Generating coherent long-form video or audio content remains a challenge for end-to-end models due to data scarcity and computational limits. Extending RAG's proven paradigm to these modalities can enable more data-efficient, controllable, and factually grounded generation for applications like video editing, sound design, and educational content creation.
proposals.hypothesis: A retrieval-augmented framework that learns implicit, cross-modal queries from video/audio context can effectively retrieve relevant multi-modal evidence, leading to higher fidelity and more contextually coherent generation than end-to-end models trained from scratch.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Implicit Query mechanism from ImpRAG to multi-modal inputs. A dual-encoder architecture will map video (via pre-trained ViT or TimeSformer features) and audio (via Wav2Vec2 features) context into a shared latent space to retrieve relevant clips from a web-scale corpus of paired video-audio-text data. For generation, we will extend the Bridge-Coder transfer approach, fine-tuning a pre-trained video (e.g., VideoGPT) or audio (e.g., Jukebox) diffusion model conditioned on the retrieved evidence, using a SCRIBES-inspired reinforcement learning objective to align generated sequences with high-level script or descriptive text constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for retrieval-augmented video and audio generation, demonstrating improved coherence and factual grounding. It will provide new benchmarks for multi-modal RAG evaluation and insights into transferring capabilities from high-resource (text, images) to low-resource (video, audio) modalities via retrieval.
proposals.novelty: While RAG is established in NLP, its application to direct video/audio generation with implicit, cross-modal query learning is unexplored. The integration of transfer learning from high-resource modalities and RL-based alignment for sequential multi-modal output constitutes a significant architectural advance.

proposals.title: MEMORYSCAPE: Scaling and Diversifying External Memory for Robust Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation
proposals.motivation: Current Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, including those using implicit queries, primarily rely on web-scale text corpora as their external memory. This limits their ability to handle complex, multimodal tasks requiring information from diverse sources like scientific figures, code repositories, structured tables, and instructional videos. As LLMs become more multimodal, the bottleneck shifts from model capacity to the quality and diversity of the retrievable knowledge base.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting RAG systems with a larger-scale, intentionally diverse multimodal memory—spanning text, code, tables, and images—will significantly improve performance on complex, composite tasks compared to systems using only web-text memory, by providing more relevant and complementary evidence.
proposals.method: We will construct MEMORYSCAPE, a massive, curated multimodal corpus integrating datasets like LAION for images, The Stack for code, WikiTables for structured data, and scientific PDF extracts. Building on the Implicit Query framework from ImpRAG, we will develop a unified, modality-agnostic retriever trained via contrastive learning to map implicit model states to relevant memory chunks across modalities. For generation, we will extend a multimodal LLM (e.g., Flamingo or GPT-4V) with a cross-attention mechanism over retrieved, encoded multimodal memories, using reinforcement learning (inspired by SCRIBES) to optimize for final task accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a systematic study of the impact of memory scale and diversity on RAG performance, 2) a novel, scalable architecture for implicit retrieval over multimodal memories, and 3) a new benchmark for evaluating multimodal RAG on tasks requiring synthesis of information from different modalities.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales memory size or uses implicit queries, MEMORYSCAPE is the first to explicitly engineer and study the *diversity* of memory modalities as a key variable for advancing RAG, moving beyond a homogeneous text-only paradigm.

proposals.title: M3-ICL: Measuring and Modeling Multimodal In-Context Learning in Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large multimodal models (LMMs) exhibit emergent in-context learning (ICL) from demonstrations, but its systematic characterization lags far behind text-only LLMs. Understanding the capabilities, scaling laws, and failure modes of multimodal ICL is critical for developing efficient, adaptable models that can learn new tasks without extensive retraining, a core challenge in pre-2026 multimodal AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multimodal ICL effectiveness is governed by a quantifiable interplay between demonstration modality (image, text, or both), task complexity, and model scale, and that its limits can be predicted by novel metrics of cross-modal alignment in the demonstration sequence.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled empirical study using established vision-language architectures (e.g., Flamingo, BLIP-2). We will design a benchmark of novel tasks requiring compositional reasoning across vision and language, systematically varying demonstration format, number, and order. Inspired by the researcher's work on implicit queries and script-based extraction, we will develop a probing framework to analyze the latent retrieval and fusion mechanisms activated during ICL, measuring attention patterns and intermediate representations to model the underlying process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive taxonomy of multimodal ICL behavior, establish scaling laws for cross-modal few-shot learning, and release a diagnostic benchmark. It will offer practical guidelines for constructing effective multimodal prompts and inform the design of more sample-efficient multimodal training paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While text-based ICL is well-studied, a systematic, mechanistic analysis of ICL with interleaved image-text demonstrations is novel. Our approach moves beyond performance reporting to model the internal retrieval and reasoning processes, bridging the researcher's expertise in retrieval-augmented generation with emerging multimodal learning.

proposals.title: Dense Multimodal Alignment for Cross-Modal Retrieval (DMACR)
proposals.motivation: Multimodal retrieval, crucial for tasks like visual question answering and image captioning, often relies on sparse or late-fusion retrieval methods that struggle with semantic alignment between text and images. Dense retrieval has revolutionized text-based QA by enabling efficient, semantic similarity search, but its application to multimodal contexts remains underexplored, limiting retrieval accuracy for complex, open-domain queries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting and extending dense passage retrieval (DPR) techniques to jointly encode text and images into a shared, high-dimensional embedding space will significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of retrieving relevant images given a textual query, and vice-versa, compared to existing sparse or dual-encoder baselines.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DPR framework by using a dual-encoder architecture with a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP or ALIGN) as a foundation. We will fine-tune the text and image encoders using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on large-scale image-text pairs (e.g., from LAION or Conceptual Captions). Crucially, we will incorporate hard negative mining strategies from text DPR and explore techniques for generating implicit, dense query representations from complex textual descriptions, inspired by the researcher's work on ImpRAG, to better capture query intent.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable framework for dense multimodal retrieval, demonstrating measurable gains in recall and precision on established benchmarks like MS-COCO and Flickr30k. It will also contribute a deeper understanding of how to effectively transfer dense retrieval principles from the textual to the multimodal domain.
proposals.novelty: While dense retrieval is established for text, its principled adaptation to multimodal settings is non-trivial. The novelty lies in the systematic integration of hard negative mining and implicit query modeling from text-based DPR and RAG research into a unified multimodal retrieval system, moving beyond simple pre-trained model embeddings.

proposals.title: REAL: Reinforcement Learning for End-to-End Adaptive Retrieval in RAG Systems
proposals.motivation: Current Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems often use a static retriever, trained on query-document relevance, which can be misaligned with the generator's downstream task performance. This leads to suboptimal document selection and limits the overall quality of generated outputs. An adaptive retriever, directly optimized for the final generation objective, is needed to close this gap and improve system coherence and accuracy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a dense passage retriever with a reinforcement learning objective, where the reward is derived from the quality of the generator's output, will create a more task-aligned retrieval system and lead to superior end-to-end RAG performance compared to independently trained retrievers.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, we initialize a bi-encoder retriever and a generator (e.g., a T5 or GPT-style model). Second, we freeze the generator and fine-tune the retriever using a policy gradient method (e.g., REINFORCE or PPO). The reward signal will be a composite metric evaluating the generator's output, combining task-specific scores (e.g., answer F1 for QA, code execution accuracy for programming) with a consistency measure between the generated text and the retrieved evidence. The retriever's action is selecting a document, and its policy is parameterized by its encoder.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel end-to-end training paradigm for RAG that directly optimizes retrieval for generation quality. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of RL-aligned retrieval across diverse NLP tasks and offer insights into the design of effective, non-differentiable reward functions for joint retrieval-generation systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like ORQA and DPR trains retrievers on heuristics like inverse cloze, and some RAG variants fine-tune components jointly via gradient flow, using RL to train the retriever based on a holistic, task-specific generator reward is a distinct and less explored direction, building directly on the team's expertise in RL for extraction (SCRIBES) and RAG (ImpRAG).

proposals.title: InverseRAG: Learning to Retrieve via Controlled Generation of Implicit Queries
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems rely on retrieving relevant documents to improve generation quality, but the inverse problem—using a generator to formulate optimal queries for retrieval—remains underexplored. This is critical for tasks requiring precise information recall from large corpora, such as long-form question answering or fact verification, where the initial user query may be ambiguous or incomplete. An effective solution would enable more robust and self-improving RAG systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A generator, conditioned on a target document and the retrieval corpus, can be trained to produce implicit queries that reliably retrieve that specific document. This learned inverse mapping is more effective for targeted recall than standard query reformulation or expansion techniques.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage training framework. First, we fine-tune a pre-trained language model (e.g., T5 or a decoder-only LLM) using a contrastive objective: given a document-query pair from a dataset like MS MARCO, the model generates a synthetic query, and we optimize to maximize the rank of the original document when this query is used by a frozen retriever (e.g., DPR or Contriever). Second, we integrate this 'Inverse Generator' into a RAG loop, using reinforcement learning (inspired by SCRIBES and ImpRAG) to refine query generation based on downstream task reward (e.g., answer accuracy).
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel paradigm for 'retrieval from generation,' providing a trainable method for creating precision-focused retrieval queries. It will advance the controllability and robustness of RAG systems, particularly for knowledge-intensive tasks. We will release a model and benchmark for targeted document recall.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses generation for query expansion or uses retrievers to aid generation, we explicitly train a generator to solve the inverse problem of finding a known document, creating an implicit, optimized query. This flips the standard RAG causality and leverages the generator's parametric knowledge to guide retrieval.

proposals.title: RAG Without Generation: Evaluating the Necessity of LLM-Based Query Diversification for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems often employ a query-rewriting or query-generation step using a Large Language Model (LLM) to produce multiple, diverse queries from a user's original input, aiming to improve retrieval recall. This generative step adds significant computational cost and latency. Given the success of simpler, non-generative retrieval augmentation methods in prior work (e.g., query expansion techniques from classical IR), it is crucial to rigorously test whether the gains from generative query diversification are necessary or if they can be matched by more efficient, deterministic methods.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance gains of RAG systems attributed to LLM-based generative query diversification can be largely replicated or even surpassed by simpler, non-generative methods for creating query variations, especially in knowledge-dense, fact-seeking tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled ablation study within a RAG pipeline similar to the Implicit Query framework from 'ImpRAG'. For a given user query, we will compare the standard approach of using an LLM to generate N diverse queries against several non-generative baselines: 1) Synonym-based expansion using static knowledge bases (e.g., WordNet) and contextual embeddings (e.g., BERT), 2) Simple paraphrasing via back-translation, and 3) Decomposing complex queries into constituent sub-questions using pre-defined, rule-based syntactic parses. We will evaluate these methods on established knowledge-intensive QA benchmarks (e.g., HotpotQA, Natural Questions) using standard retrieval (e.g., DPR, Contriever) and answer generation metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence quantifying the marginal utility of generative query diversification in RAG. It will establish a strong, efficient baseline for query variation, potentially simplifying RAG system design. The findings could lead to more cost-effective and deployable RAG systems, particularly for latency-sensitive applications.
proposals.novelty: While query expansion is a classic IR technique, its systematic comparison and potential to replace a now-standard LLM-based component in modern RAG pipelines is underexplored. This directly challenges a growing assumption in post-2020 RAG research that LLM-based generation is essential for effective query reformulation.

proposals.title: TailRetriever: Self-Supervised Dense Retrieval for Long-Tail Entities via In-Domain Contrastive Alignment
proposals.motivation: Dense retrievers often underperform on long-tail entity queries due to a lack of supervised training data for obscure entities. Existing methods rely on cross-encoder re-rankers for supervision or synthetic data from large language models (LLMs), which are computationally expensive and can propagate biases. A method to train high-quality retrievers directly for niche domains without such external supervision is a critical open problem in robust information access.
proposals.hypothesis: High-quality positive and hard negative training pairs for long-tail entities can be mined directly from an in-domain corpus via structured data extraction and entity-attribute co-occurrence analysis, enabling effective self-supervised contrastive training of a specialized dense retriever.
proposals.method: We will adapt techniques from SCRIBES to extract (entity, attribute, value) tuples from semi-structured web documents about obscure entities. Using these tuples, we will generate query-document positive pairs (e.g., query: 'Xylophagia cause', document: containing 'Xylophagia' and 'psychological condition'). Hard negatives will be mined via in-batch sampling and by identifying documents sharing common attribute types but different values. A bi-encoder (e.g., a pre-trained transformer) will be trained using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on these self-generated pairs to align queries and relevant documents in the embedding space.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a computationally efficient, domain-specialized dense retriever that excels at long-tail queries without cross-encoder supervision. It will advance methods for self-supervised retrieval and provide a blueprint for adapting retrieval systems to data-scarce domains. The approach also connects structured data extraction directly to representation learning for retrieval.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior self-supervised methods that rely on external models or generic augmentations, our approach generates high-quality, in-domain supervision signals by programmatically analyzing the inherent structure and co-occurrence patterns within the target corpus itself, specifically tailored for long-tail entities.

proposals.title: Adaptive Landscape Sampling for Efficient Augmentation Strategy Optimization in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Diverse data augmentation is crucial for improving LLM robustness and generalization, but exhaustively searching the vast combinatorial space of augmentation techniques and parameters is computationally intractable for models with billions of parameters. Current methods like the DRAGON framework are effective for smaller models but do not scale efficiently. An efficient, principled method for navigating this 'augmentation landscape' is needed to make diverse augmentation feasible for state-of-the-art LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the space of augmentation strategies as a high-dimensional fitness landscape and applying adaptive sampling techniques from population genetics, we can efficiently identify optimal augmentation combinations for large LLMs, achieving superior robustness-generalization trade-offs compared to random or heuristic search.
proposals.method: We will formalize an augmentation performance landscape where dimensions are parameters for techniques like synonym replacement, back-translation, and template-based generation. Using a proxy model (e.g., a distilled version of the target LLM) or early training checkpoints, we will estimate performance gradients. We will implement an adaptive sampling algorithm, inspired by population genetics' adaptive walks, that maintains a diverse population of augmentation strategies, selectively explores promising regions, and uses a multi-armed bandit or evolutionary strategy to balance exploration and exploitation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, computationally efficient framework for applying diverse augmentation to large LLMs, directly improving their robustness on tasks like question answering and generation. It will also contribute a novel methodology for hyperparameter and strategy optimization in high-dimensional, non-differentiable spaces by bridging concepts from computational biology.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct conceptual and algorithmic transfer of adaptive landscape sampling from population genetics to LLM augmentation optimization, a connection not explored in prior ML work. This moves beyond simple grid or random search and heuristic selection of augmentations.

proposals.title: Streaming Fact Verification via Adaptive Query Augmentation and Dynamic RAG
proposals.motivation: The velocity of misinformation on social media and news platforms necessitates real-time fact-checking systems. Existing RAG-based verification models rely on static knowledge indices and single-query retrieval, making them slow to incorporate new evidence and brittle to nuanced claim phrasing. This gap prevents reliable verification in dynamic information environments where both claims and evidence evolve rapidly.
proposals.hypothesis: A system that adaptively generates diverse query reformulations for each claim and executes them against a continuously updated, streaming RAG index will achieve higher accuracy and lower latency in real-time fact verification compared to systems using static augmentation or static knowledge alone.
proposals.method: We will build a two-stream architecture. For the query stream, we will use an LLM (e.g., GPT-4) fine-tuned with reinforcement learning (inspired by SCRIBES) to generate multiple, diverse reformulations of an incoming claim, optimizing for retrieval diversity. For the evidence stream, we will implement a second-scale streaming algorithm (e.g., a reservoir sampling variant) to continuously update a dense vector index with new documents. A fusion module, informed by retrieval confidence scores and temporal relevance, will aggregate evidence from all augmented queries to produce a final verification judgment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for high-velocity fact verification that jointly optimizes for query diversity and knowledge freshness, 2) a new benchmark for streaming fact verification derived from real-time social media data, and 3) empirical analysis of the trade-offs between augmentation diversity, update latency, and verification accuracy.
proposals.novelty: While query augmentation and streaming updates exist separately, their fusion for real-time verification is unexplored. The key novelty is the closed-loop, adaptive generation of diverse queries specifically conditioned on a dynamically changing evidence base, moving beyond static RAG or batch-oriented systems.

proposals.title: Diverse Augmentation for Robust Retrieval: Generalizing to Open-Domain QA and Fact Verification
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems are critical for grounding large language models (LLMs) in external knowledge, but their performance is highly sensitive to the quality and diversity of retrieved passages. Prior work, including the researcher's own ImpRAG, has shown the value of generating diverse implicit queries for script-based extraction. However, it remains unclear whether this diverse augmentation principle generalizes to core knowledge-intensive NLP tasks like open-domain question answering (QA) and fact verification, where retrieval failures directly propagate to generation errors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly promoting diversity in the retrieved context—through multi-query generation and controlled augmentation—will significantly improve the robustness and final answer accuracy of RAG systems on open-domain QA and fact verification, compared to standard single-query retrieval.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the diverse augmentation framework from SCRIBES and ImpRAG. For a given input (question or claim), a fine-tuned T5 or a prompted LLM (e.g., GPT-3.5) will generate multiple, semantically distinct query variants. These queries will be executed in parallel against a dense passage retriever (like DPR or Contriever). We will then explore fusion strategies, including a learned reranker (e.g., a cross-encoder) and a novel diversity-aware aggregation module, to synthesize a consolidated, non-redundant context for the generator. The full pipeline will be evaluated on established benchmarks: Natural Questions (open-domain QA) and FEVER (fact verification).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic evaluation of diverse augmentation for two fundamental retrieval tasks, establishing its generalizability beyond data extraction. It will deliver a modular, task-agnostic framework for robust retrieval and release code and model checkpoints to facilitate further research in reliable RAG.
proposals.novelty: While multi-query retrieval has been explored, its integration with a learned, diversity-focused synthesis mechanism for QA and verification is novel. This moves beyond simple query expansion by explicitly modeling and optimizing for informational diversity in the final context presented to the generator.

proposals.title: SAPO: State-Adaptive Policy Optimization for Online Data Augmentation
proposals.motivation: Data augmentation is crucial for training robust deep learning models, but existing methods rely on static policies or offline search, which are suboptimal as the model's learning dynamics change throughout training. This inefficiency is particularly acute in low-resource settings and for complex tasks like code generation and information extraction, where the value of an augmentation depends heavily on the model's current state. An online, adaptive system is needed to maximize training signal and robustness per epoch.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating augmentation selection as a contextual bandit problem, where the context is a compact representation of the model's instantaneous learning state, will allow for dynamic policy optimization that yields more sample-efficient training and improved final model robustness compared to static or pre-computed policies.
proposals.method: We will develop a lightweight meta-controller, implemented as a small neural network, that observes a context vector comprising batch statistics (e.g., loss, gradient norms), model state indicators (e.g., layer-wise activations), and task-specific signals. This controller will select from a discrete set of augmentation operations (or continuous parameters) for each batch. We will train the controller using a policy gradient method (e.g., REINFORCE) or a hyperparameter response model, with a reward signal based on the reduction in the primary model's loss on a held-out validation set. The system will be integrated into the training loop of a transformer-based LLM.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for online, state-aware data augmentation that directly optimizes for training progress. It will provide a practical method to improve sample efficiency and adversarial robustness in tasks like code generation and structured data extraction. The research will also advance the understanding of how to use meta-learning for real-time hyperparameter adaptation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike AutoAugment or RandAugment, which search for a fixed policy, SAPO continuously adapts the policy based on the live training context. It also differs from adversarial training by focusing on general, task-relevant augmentations guided by an online optimization objective rather than worst-case perturbations.

proposals.title: Manifold Mixup for Dense Retrievers: Improving Generalization via Embedding Space Interpolation
proposals.motivation: Dense retrieval models, while powerful, often suffer from overfitting to spurious patterns in limited training data, leading to suboptimal generalization on out-of-distribution queries and passages. Data augmentation techniques common in computer vision, like mixup, are underexplored for text representation learning, particularly in the embedding spaces of dual-encoder architectures. This project investigates whether interpolative augmentation can create a smoother, more robust embedding manifold, addressing a key bottleneck in retrieval model robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying mixup or manifold mixup in the latent space of a dense retriever's encoder during training will regularize the model, leading to a more continuous and generalizable semantic embedding space, thereby improving retrieval accuracy and robustness on diverse and unseen queries.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Manifold Mixup algorithm to the contrastive learning framework of dense retrievers like DPR or ANCE. During training, for a given batch of query and positive passage embeddings generated by the encoder, we will randomly select pairs and perform convex interpolation (lambda * embedding_i + (1-lambda) * embedding_j) between them, applying the same mixing coefficient lambda to their corresponding contrastive loss labels. We will experiment with mixing at different encoder layers and explore strategies for interpolating within query-passage positive pairs versus across different instances. The model will be evaluated on standard retrieval benchmarks (e.g., MS MARCO, BEIR) for in-domain and zero-shot out-of-domain performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of interpolation-based data augmentation for dense retrieval, establishing a novel regularization technique. It will yield insights into the geometry of learned retrieval embedding spaces and deliver a simple, plug-in training method to improve model generalization without architectural changes or additional data.
proposals.novelty: While mixup is established in vision, its application to the embedding space of contrastive text retrieval models is novel. The key innovation is adapting the interpolation to the paired (query, passage) structure and the contrastive loss objective of retrieval, rather than standard classification.

proposals.title: RAG-Time: Adversarial Temporal Hardening for Robust Retrieval of High-Velocity Facts
proposals.motivation: Real-time information like stock prices or sports scores changes on a second-to-minute timescale, creating a critical challenge for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. Standard RAG, built on static corpora, fails in this dynamic setting as its retriever cannot reliably distinguish the current factual state from a very recent, outdated one, leading to high-stakes errors. This work addresses the core robustness gap in applying RAG to live, high-velocity data streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a retriever can be made robust to the high-velocity fact landscape by adversarially training it to become invariant to noisy user queries while remaining hypersensitive to fine-grained semantic and temporal signals that differentiate the current state from recent past states.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage adversarial training framework. First, we generate 'temporal confounders' by sampling realistic but outdated facts from a timestamped high-velocity stream (e.g., a live sports ticker). Second, we jointly train a dense passage retriever (DPR) against an adversary that perturbs queries with synthetic noise and selects these confounders. The retriever's objective is to correctly retrieve the current fact while maximizing the margin from the top adversarial confounder, using a contrastive loss enhanced with temporal distance penalties. The generator-adversary is trained with a reinforcement learning objective, inspired by SCRIBES, to find challenging negative examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel adversarial training paradigm specifically for temporal robustness in retrieval, 2) a benchmark for evaluating RAG on high-velocity factual queries, and 3) a hardened retriever model that maintains accuracy with messy inputs and dynamic knowledge, advancing reliable real-time question answering.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training exists for static domains, applying it to create temporal invariance for second-scale fact shifts is novel. This approach uniquely frames the retrieval challenge as discriminating between nearly identical facts separated only by minimal time, moving beyond simple query augmentation or frequent index updates.

proposals.title: CL-DBE: A Symbiotic Curriculum Learning and Dynamic Benchmark Evolution Framework for Robust RAG
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) models are static, quickly becoming obsolete as models improve and real-world data distributions shift. This creates a gap between measured performance and a model's ability to handle novel, complex reasoning in dynamic environments. A benchmark that actively evolves to challenge the frontier of model capabilities is crucial for driving progress in robust, real-world AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop system, where a benchmark's difficulty curriculum is dynamically generated based on a model's performance profile and real-world data shifts, will accelerate model training on complex reasoning and produce a more robust, pedagogically structured evaluation suite than static benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will build upon the CRAG benchmark and ImpRAG's implicit query framework. The core is a curriculum controller using a reinforcement learning policy (inspired by SCRIBES) to select the next task or domain from a generator. This generator creates new questions by perturbing CRAG data and integrating web-scale, semi-structured data streams. The controller's reward is based on the model's performance trajectory, encouraging sequences that maximize learning progress. The trained model's weaknesses then inform new benchmark generation, creating an adaptive loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel framework for dynamic, curriculum-driven benchmark evolution, 2) a methodology for training RAG models on adaptive difficulty progressions, and 3) an open-source system that produces both stronger models and a more challenging, continuously updated evaluation suite for complex reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and RAG benchmarks exist separately, their integration into a symbiotic, closed-loop system where the benchmark actively learns to challenge the model is novel. This moves beyond static evaluation towards an active, pedagogical partnership between training and assessment.

proposals.title: MUSE-RAG: Multi-Teacher Distillation for Synthesis and Judgment in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
proposals.motivation: Current RAG systems excel at factual question-answering but struggle with open-ended tasks like creative brainstorming or multi-step planning, which require synthesis and judgment rather than simple fact lookup. Pre-2026, training RAG for these non-factoid tasks is hindered by a lack of high-quality labeled datasets and the complexity of defining a single supervision signal.
