ideas.title: Adaptive Caching and Invalidation for RAG using a CDN-like Freshness Layer
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) and web caching—which efficiently manage rapidly changing content like news and stock prices—to the problem of RAG for highly dynamic facts. The analogous solution uses hierarchical caching with precise invalidation rules (TTL, ETags) and real-time origin pushes. Adapted to RAG, the idea is to develop a system with a 'Freshness Layer' that acts as a smart, query-aware cache for retrieved facts. Each fact is annotated with a domain-specific volatility score or TTL. For user queries, the system first checks this layer; if the cached fact is stale or absent, it triggers a prioritized, real-time search or subscribes to push updates from live data streams. This ensures answers to questions about facts changing in seconds or minutes are both fresh and efficient, balancing latency with the computational cost of constant index updates.

ideas.title: Adapting Metabolic Pathway Analysis for Multi-Hop RAG Reasoning
ideas.description: This idea addresses complex multi-hop reasoning in RAG by drawing an analogy from Systems Biology. In metabolic engineering, scientists solve the problem of synthesizing a complex compound by analyzing predefined biochemical networks (pathways), identifying necessary intermediate substrates and the enzymes that transform them. The analogous solution involves mapping these multi-step processes using constraint-based models to ensure viability. Adapted to RAG, the research idea is to design systems that treat reasoning questions as pathways. The system would explicitly model the reasoning chain needed, proactively retrieve and validate intermediate 'factual substrates' from multiple sources, and assemble them into a verified logical sequence. This pathway-aware retrieval and synthesis would make answering questions requiring synthesis across several documents more reliable by ensuring each logical 'hop' is supported and connected.

ideas.description: This system employs an ensemble of diverse teachers but adds a trainable gating network that assesses the context and confidence of each teacher's output for every input instance. The gating mechanism dynamically assigns weights or masks unreliable teachers, ensuring the student model primarily learns from coherent, high-agreement pseudo-labels. This approach mitigates the noise introduced by teacher disagreements, leading to more stable and robust distillation, especially on challenging or out-of-domain data.

ideas.title: Synthesize Reasoning Plans via Chemical Retrosynthesis Analogy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving framework of retrosynthetic analysis from organic chemistry to the domain of complex Question Answering (QA). In retrosynthesis, chemists start with a complex target molecule and work backwards, applying known reaction rules to break it down into simpler, available building blocks, forming a verifiable synthesis plan. Analogously, for complex QA, we treat the final, complex answer as the 'target'. A program synthesis engine, equipped with a library of validated reasoning 'reaction' templates (e.g., logical deduction, comparative analysis, mathematical calculation), works backwards from this answer. It recursively decomposes the question into a tree of simpler, executable sub-queries (the 'precursors') whose answers can be reliably retrieved or computed from the RAG knowledge base. This generates a complete, multi-step reasoning plan that is verifiable for correctness and feasibility before execution, thereby increasing the reliability and transparency of the reasoning process in RAG systems.

ideas.title: Apply Stratigraphic Layering from Geology to Temporal RAG
ideas.description: This idea adapts the stratigraphic layering principle from geology to solve the problem of managing temporally-scoped facts in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). In geology, strata provide a clear, sequential record of history. Analogously, a Temporal RAG system would organize its embedded knowledge into chronological layers or versions. Each piece of ingested text (e.g., a news article, a scientific abstract, a company report) is timestamped and embedded, forming a 'knowledge stratum.' During retrieval, a user's query is coupled with a specific temporal context (e.g., 'as of December 2020'). The system then retrieves information only from strata that were deposited (published) and were valid (not yet superseded) at that point in the specified timeline. This prevents temporal contradictions and anachronisms, allowing the LLM to generate answers that are accurate within the historical context, much like a geologist interpreting the Earth's past from a specific layer of rock.

ideas.title: Beyond Factual Retrieval: Adapting Ecological Niche Theory to Map RAG's Latent Capabilities
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that factual QA is RAG's primary use case. By drawing an analogy from ecology's 'fundamental vs. realized niche' theory, it proposes a novel investigative framework. In ecology, an organism's full potential (fundamental niche) is often obscured in nature by competition, and is revealed through controlled experiments. Similarly, RAG's current dominance in factual QA may be its 'realized niche' shaped by initial research focus and evaluation metrics, potentially hiding a broader 'fundamental niche' of capabilities. The adapted idea is to systematically design and run benchmark tasks for creative synthesis, complex planning, and conceptual blending in a controlled 'competitor-free' environment—i.e., without the default objective of factual accuracy—to experimentally map the full, latent functional space of RAG architectures.

ideas.title: Fault Injection for Realistic API Simulation
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that mock web/Knowledge Graph APIs fully capture real-world retrieval noise and failures by drawing an analogy from fault tolerance in distributed systems. In that field, Chaos Engineering and fault injection are used to proactively test system resilience by simulating realistic failures. Adapting this solution, we propose developing a configurable fault-injection framework for mock APIs. This framework would enable the simulation of a wide range of realistic retrieval pathologies—such as transient errors, degraded performance, and inconsistent data—thereby providing a much more robust and truthful testbed for evaluating the robustness of applications that depend on these APIs.

ideas.title: Expanding CRAG's Domain Taxonomy via Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from ecology, where the shift from rigid niche categories to functional trait-based analysis solved the problem of capturing biodiversity complexity. The analogous problem in CRAG research is the assumption that its five domains comprehensively categorize all real-world questions, excluding areas like legal or personal domains. By adapting the ecological solution, we propose to challenge this assumption by developing a functional trait framework for question analysis. This involves deconstructing questions into fundamental, measurable dimensions (e.g., deductive vs. interpretive reasoning, reliance on statutory vs. empirical evidence, personal vs. societal stakes). Mapping a wide corpus of questions onto this multi-dimensional space would empirically demonstrate that CRAG's five-domain taxonomy is an incomplete projection of a richer continuum, thereby arguing for a more expansive and flexible model of question domains.

ideas.title: Achieve fully trustworthy QA via RAG with Formal Verification
ideas.description: This idea bridges the gap to 100% hallucination-free QA by drawing an analogy from formal methods in hardware verification. In that field, mathematical proofs guarantee a chip's behavior matches its specification for all inputs, providing absolute certainty. We adapt this by defining a formal specification for a trustworthy RAG answer (e.g., provenance, entailment, lack of contradiction). The research involves developing a lightweight, automated verification layer for the RAG pipeline that can mathematically prove an answer's adherence to this specification based on the retrieved contexts, thereby providing a verifiable certificate of trustworthiness for each response, moving beyond probabilistic confidence.

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Trial Endpoints for Holistic RAG Evaluation
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption that accuracy and hallucination rates are sufficient metrics for RAG systems, proposing the addition of metrics like efficiency, latency, and cost for real-world deployment. Drawing an analogy from clinical medicine, the solution in drug development is to mandate a comprehensive set of endpoints beyond primary efficacy for regulatory approval, including quality of life, treatment burden, and safety. Adapting this, the research idea proposes a holistic evaluation framework for RAG systems where a 'deployment dossier' must include mandatory secondary metrics profiling operational characteristics—such as computational efficiency (FLOPS, memory), latency (P50, P99 response times), and cost-per-query—alongside traditional accuracy metrics. This framework treats these operational metrics as the system's 'safety and tolerability' profile, essential for assessing real-world viability, just as side effects and administration burden are critical for a drug's approval and use.

ideas.title: Apply Adaptive Scaffolding from Educational Psychology to RL Curriculum Learning
ideas.description: This idea formalizes progressive training in RL as an adaptive scaffolding problem, drawing a direct analogy from educational psychology. The analogous solution is the dynamic, assessment-driven adjustment of support (scaffolds) based on a learner's Zone of Proximal Development. Adapted to RL, this involves creating a meta-learning framework that continuously evaluates an agent's capabilities, diagnoses its weaknesses, and automatically sequences or generates training tasks. It also dynamically adjusts auxiliary aids like reward shaping, action constraints, or simplified environment dynamics, which are systematically faded out as the agent masters subtasks, thereby automating the construction of an optimal, personalized curriculum for complex skill acquisition.
